summary:
China's economic reform program, initiated from 1978 onward under [[event:3650|Deng Xiaoping's leadership]], transformed the world's most populous nation from a centrally planned economy into a market-oriented system and generated one of the most dramatic episodes of economic growth in recorded history. The reforms began cautiously in agriculture, replacing collective farming with a household responsibility system that allowed farmers to sell surplus produce at market prices. Agricultural output rose sharply, releasing rural labor for industrial and urban employment.

The opening of special economic zones — particularly in southern China — attracted foreign investment and created laboratories for market-oriented policies. Township and village enterprises expanded non-state industrial production. Gradually the state sector was restructured, prices were liberalized, and China began integrating into global trade networks. The reforms proceeded pragmatically, under the guiding philosophy of 'crossing the river by feeling for the stones' — testing and adjusting policies based on results rather than ideological blueprints.

The results were extraordinary. China's GDP grew at an average rate of approximately ten percent annually for three decades, lifting hundreds of millions of people out of poverty in the largest reduction in poverty in human history. By the early twenty-first century China had become the world's second-largest economy and the largest manufacturer and exporter globally.

The [[event:2690|Tiananmen Square protests]] of 1989 posed the most serious political challenge to the reform era. The government's violent suppression of the protests preserved political stability at the cost of democratic political reform, establishing the model of economic liberalization without political liberalization that has characterized China ever since. The tension between economic openness and political control remains central to understanding contemporary China.
